---
title: "LeetCode 1047. Remove All Adjacent Duplicates In String"
subtitle: "Remove All Adjacent Duplicates In String"
lang: en
date: 2026-07-18
author: Anthony
categories: [LeetCode, String, Stack]
description: "[Problem link](https://leetcode.com/problems/remove-all-adjacent-duplicates-in-string/description/)"
image: ../../cover/Leetcode.svg
alternate-lang: "/dsa/posts/leetcode/1047-remove-adjacent-duplicates/1047-remove-adjacent-duplicates.html"
---

You are given a string `s` consisting of lowercase English letters. This kind of duplicate removal[^1] is carried out on `s` repeatedly, until it can no longer be done, and the final string after all duplicate removals is returned — it can be proven that the answer is unique.

For example, given

```
s = "abbaca"
```

we can first remove the adjacent pair `"bb"`, leaving `"aaca"`. That leaves a new adjacent pair `"aa"`, which we remove as well, leaving `"ca"` — which can no longer be reduced, so the answer is `"ca"`.

## Approach

This problem has a lot in common with [LeetCode 20. Valid Parentheses](../0020-valid-parentheses/0020-valid-parentheses-en.qmd): both scan the string while comparing the current character against the top of a stack, and cancel the two out whenever a condition is met — in Valid Parentheses that's a matching bracket pair, here it's two equal adjacent letters. Whenever the condition doesn't hold, the current character is pushed onto the stack and scanning continues.

By the end of the scan, whatever is left on the stack is exactly the final string after all duplicate removals.

## Implementation

::: {.panel-tabset}
## C

```c
char *removeDuplicates(char *s)
{
    char *stack = (char *)malloc((strlen(s) + 1) * sizeof(char));
    int count = 0;

    for (size_t i = 0; i < strlen(s); i++)
    {
        if (count > 0 && stack[count - 1] == s[i])
        {
            // Cancels out with the top of the stack
            count--;
        }
        else
        {
            // No match, push onto the stack
            stack[count++] = s[i];
        }
    }

    stack[count] = '\0';
    return stack;
}
```

## C++

```cpp
class Solution
{
public:
    string removeDuplicates(string s)
    {
        string stack;

        for (auto ch : s)
        {
            if (!stack.empty() && ch == stack.back())
                // Cancels out with the top of the stack
                stack.pop_back();
            else
                // No match, push onto the stack
                stack.push_back(ch);
        }

        return stack;
    }
};
```

## Python

```python
class Solution:
    def removeDuplicates(self, s: str) -> str:
        stack = []

        for ch in s:
            if stack and stack[-1] == ch:
                # Cancels out with the top of the stack
                stack.pop()
            else:
                # No match, push onto the stack
                stack.append(ch)

        return ''.join(stack)
```
:::

[^1]: A **duplicate removal** means selecting two letters that are adjacent and identical, and removing both of them.
